Chapter 1  —  Electrostatics

Electromagnetic Field Theory

A complete visual guide to electrostatics — from Coulomb's Law to Maxwell's Equations

$$\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$$
$$\oint_C \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{S}$$
$$\oint_S \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{S} = 0$$
$$\oint_C \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{encl} + \mu_0\varepsilon_0\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{S}$$

Contents

L-02

Introduction & Coulomb's Law

What is This Course?

This course provides a fundamental understanding of how electricity and magnetism work. The goal is to understand Maxwell's four equations — one by one, piece by piece:

  • How these equations describe electric and magnetic fields
  • Where those fields come from and how they are created
  • What they do — how they affect charges and currents

Why This Course?

These four equations were written down in 1864. They describe an enormous amount of natural phenomena and technology:

  • Any electrical circuit, at a fundamental level, is governed by Maxwell's equations
  • They also describe many natural phenomena — e.g., lightning
  • In 1887, Heinrich Hertz discovered for the first time the wave solutions to Maxwell's equations
  • These are the waves used by cell phones, television signals, Wi-Fi, microwaves, and light
James Clerk Maxwell (1831–1879) — who unified electricity, magnetism, and optics

Atom and Charge

  • All matter is made up of charged particles — electrons and protons
  • Electrons have negative charge; protons have positive charge
  • If an atom is neutral, the number of protons equals the number of electrons
  • Adding one electron to a neutral atom → negative ion
  • Removing one electron from a neutral atom → positive ion
Unit of Charge
  • Unit of charge is the Coulomb (C) — a very large amount of charge
  • Charge of a single electron: $-e$ where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
  • Quantized: The charge of any object is an integer multiple of $e$
  • Conserved: We cannot create charge out of nowhere; we can only separate positive and negative charges
Problem 1

Calculate the number of electrons in 1 C?

$$n = \frac{1\,\text{C}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}} = 6.24 \times 10^{18}\,\text{electrons}$$

Electric Force: Coulomb's Law

Two positive charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ separated by distance $r$. Force $\vec{F}_{1,2}$ acts along $\hat{r}_{1,2}$.

French army engineer Colonel Charles Coulomb stated that the force between two very small objects separated in vacuum (or free space) by a distance which is large compared to their size is:

Coulomb's Law (Proportionality)
$$F \propto \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2}$$
Eq. (5)

The force is Central — it points along the direction joining the two objects. The vector force on charge $Q_2$ due to $Q_1$ is:

Coulomb's Law (Vector Form)
$$\vec{F}_{12} = k\frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2}\hat{r}_{12} = k\frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r_{12}^3}\vec{r}_{12}$$ where $\hat{r}_{12}$ is the unit vector in the direction from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$, and $k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \;\text{N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$
Eq. (6), (13)–(15)
Note on the Vector Form

When writing $\vec{F}_{12} = k\dfrac{Q_1 Q_2}{r_{12}^3}\vec{r}_{12}$, the force is divided by the distance cubed because $\vec{r}_{12}$ carries one factor of $r$. The magnitude of the force still follows an inverse square law.

Electric Force vs. Gravitational Force

Electric Force (Coulomb)

$$F_e = k\frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2}, \quad k = 9\times10^9 \frac{\text{N·m}^2}{\text{C}^2}$$

Can be attractive or repulsive depending on signs of charges

Gravitational Force (Newton)

$$F_g = G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}, \quad G = 6.67\times10^{-11} \frac{\text{N·m}^2}{\text{kg}^2}$$

Always attractive — there is no negative mass

Similarities
  • Both are inverse square laws ($\propto 1/r^2$)
  • The units of $k$ and $G$ are similar
Problem 3 — Two Protons

Consider two protons separated by $10^{-12}$ m. Calculate the ratio of electric force to gravitational force. (Mass of proton = $1.7 \times 10^{-27}$ kg)

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} \approx 10^{36}$$

Electric force is $10^{36}$ times stronger than gravitational force between protons!

Problem 4 — Earth and Mars

Earth and Mars each have a charge of $4 \times 10^5$ C. With mass of Earth = $5.972 \times 10^{24}$ kg and mass of Mars = $6.39 \times 10^{23}$ kg, calculate the ratio of electric force to gravitational force.

$$\frac{F_e}{F_g} \approx 10^{-17} \implies F_g = 10^{17} \cdot F_e$$

At the planetary scale, gravity dominates over electric forces by a factor of $10^{17}$.

Scale Dependence of Forces
  • On an atomic scale, it is the electric forces that hold our world together
  • On a cosmic scale (planets, stars, galaxies), it is gravity that governs structure
  • Although our immediate surroundings are dominated by electric forces, the behaviour of the universe on a large scale is dictated by gravity
L-03 / L-04 / L-05

Coordinate Systems

Overview of Coordinate Systems

A coordinate system consists of four basic elements:

  1. Choice of origin
  2. Choice of axes
  3. Choice of positive direction for each axis
  4. Choice of unit vectors for each axis

Three coordinate systems are studied in this course, all with three mutually perpendicular axes:

Cartesian: axes $x, y, z$
Cylindrical: axes $\rho, \phi, z$
Spherical: axes $r, \theta, \phi$

Vectors and Unit Vectors

A vector in any coordinate system is represented as a sum of three component vectors along the coordinate axes. Unit vectors have unit magnitude and point in the direction of increasing coordinate values.

Vector Magnitude & Unit Vector (Cartesian)
For $\mathbf{B} = B_x\hat{a}_x + B_y\hat{a}_y + B_z\hat{a}_z$: $$|\mathbf{B}| = \sqrt{B_x^2 + B_y^2 + B_z^2}$$ $$\hat{a}_B = \frac{\mathbf{B}}{|\mathbf{B}|} = \frac{B_x\hat{a}_x + B_y\hat{a}_y + B_z\hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{B_x^2 + B_y^2 + B_z^2}}$$
Eq. (9)–(12)
Problem 5

Write the vector $\mathbf{r}_P$ pointing from the origin to point $P(1,2,3)$ in Cartesian coordinates. Find its magnitude and unit vector.

$\mathbf{r}_P = \hat{a}_x + 2\hat{a}_y + 3\hat{a}_z$

$|\mathbf{r}_P| = \sqrt{1+4+9} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742$

$\hat{a}_r = \dfrac{\hat{a}_x + 2\hat{a}_y + 3\hat{a}_z}{3.742}$

Dot Product

$$\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{B} = |\mathbf{A}||\mathbf{B}|\cos\theta_{AB} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$$ Component of $\mathbf{B}$ in direction $\hat{a}$: $\;\mathbf{B}\cdot\hat{a} = |\mathbf{B}|\cos\theta_{Ba}$
Eq. (17)–(19)

Cross Product

$$\mathbf{A}\times\mathbf{B} = |\mathbf{A}||\mathbf{B}|\sin\theta_{AB}\,\hat{a}_N$$ $$\mathbf{A}\times\mathbf{B} = \begin{vmatrix}\hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z\end{vmatrix} = (A_yB_z-A_zB_y)\hat{a}_x + (A_zB_x-A_xB_z)\hat{a}_y + (A_xB_y-A_yB_x)\hat{a}_z$$
Eq. (20)–(21)
Superposition of Coulomb's Law

When more than two charges are present, the total force on any one charge is the vector sum of the forces from all others:

$$\vec{F}_3 = \vec{F}_{13} + \vec{F}_{23}$$
Eq. (16)

Coordinate System Comparison

PropertyCartesianCylindricalSpherical
Axes $x,\,y,\,z$ $\rho,\,\phi,\,z$ $r,\,\theta,\,\phi$
Unit Vectors $\hat{a}_x,\,\hat{a}_y,\,\hat{a}_z$ $\hat{a}_\rho,\,\hat{a}_\phi,\,\hat{a}_z$ $\hat{a}_r,\,\hat{a}_\theta,\,\hat{a}_\phi$
Range $-\infty < x,y,z < \infty$ $0 \le \rho < \infty$, $0 \le \phi < 2\pi$, $-\infty < z < \infty$ $0 \le r < \infty$, $0 \le \theta \le \pi$, $0 \le \phi < 2\pi$
Vector $A_x\hat{a}_x+A_y\hat{a}_y+A_z\hat{a}_z$ $A_\rho\hat{a}_\rho+A_\phi\hat{a}_\phi+A_z\hat{a}_z$ $A_r\hat{a}_r+A_\theta\hat{a}_\theta+A_\phi\hat{a}_\phi$
Magnitude $\sqrt{A_x^2+A_y^2+A_z^2}$ $\sqrt{A_\rho^2+A_\phi^2+A_z^2}$ $\sqrt{A_r^2+A_\theta^2+A_\phi^2}$
Dot (self) $\hat{a}_x\cdot\hat{a}_x=1$, $\hat{a}_x\cdot\hat{a}_y=0$ $\hat{a}_\rho\cdot\hat{a}_\rho=1$, $\hat{a}_\rho\cdot\hat{a}_\phi=0$ $\hat{a}_r\cdot\hat{a}_r=1$, $\hat{a}_r\cdot\hat{a}_\theta=0$
Cross (cyclic) $\hat{a}_x\times\hat{a}_y=\hat{a}_z$ $\hat{a}_\rho\times\hat{a}_\phi=\hat{a}_z$ $\hat{a}_r\times\hat{a}_\theta=\hat{a}_\phi$

Constant-Coordinate Surfaces

CoordinateCartesianCylindricalSpherical
1st$x=\text{const}$: Infinite plane$\rho=\text{const}$: Circular cylinder$r=\text{const}$: Sphere of radius $r$
2nd$y=\text{const}$: Infinite plane$\phi=\text{const}$: Semi-infinite plane (edge along $z$-axis)$\theta=\text{const}$: Circular cone (vertex at origin)
3rd$z=\text{const}$: Infinite plane$z=\text{const}$: Infinite plane$\phi=\text{const}$: Semi-infinite plane (edge along $z$-axis)

Differential Length, Area, and Volume

Cartesian Coordinate System

Differential Elements — Cartesian
$$d\mathbf{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$d\mathbf{S}_x = dy\,dz\,\hat{a}_x,\quad d\mathbf{S}_y = dx\,dz\,\hat{a}_y,\quad d\mathbf{S}_z = dx\,dy\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$dv = dx\,dy\,dz$$

Cylindrical Coordinate System

Differential Elements — Cylindrical
$$d\mathbf{l} = d\rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + \rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$d\mathbf{S}_\rho = \rho\,d\phi\,dz\,\hat{a}_\rho,\quad d\mathbf{S}_\phi = d\rho\,dz\,\hat{a}_\phi,\quad d\mathbf{S}_z = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$dv = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$$

Spherical Coordinate System

Differential volume element in spherical coordinates at $(r,\theta,\phi)$
Differential Elements — Spherical
$$d\mathbf{l} = dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ $$d\mathbf{S}_r = r^2\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_r,\quad d\mathbf{S}_\theta = r\sin\theta\,dr\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\theta,\quad d\mathbf{S}_\phi = r\,dr\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ $$dv = r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$$

Coordinate Transformations

Cartesian ↔ Cylindrical

Point Transformation
Cartesian → Cylindrical: $\rho = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$, $z=z$

Cylindrical → Cartesian: $x = \rho\cos\phi$, $y = \rho\sin\phi$, $z=z$
Vector Transformation (Cart → Cyl)
$$\begin{pmatrix}\hat{a}_\rho \\ \hat{a}_\phi \\ \hat{a}_z\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\phi & \sin\phi & 0 \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\hat{a}_x \\ \hat{a}_y \\ \hat{a}_z\end{pmatrix}$$

Cartesian ↔ Spherical

Point Transformation
Cartesian → Spherical:
$r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$,  $\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{r}\right)$,  $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$

Spherical → Cartesian:
$x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi$,  $y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi$,  $z = r\cos\theta$
Vector Transformation (Cart → Sph)
$$\begin{pmatrix}\hat{a}_r \\ \hat{a}_\theta \\ \hat{a}_\phi\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}\sin\theta\cos\phi & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta \\ \cos\theta\cos\phi & \cos\theta\sin\phi & -\sin\theta \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\hat{a}_x \\ \hat{a}_y \\ \hat{a}_z\end{pmatrix}$$
Problem 8 — Cylindrical Area

Use the cylindrical coordinate system to find the area of the curved surface of a right circular cylinder where $r = 2$ m, $h = 5$ m, and $30° \le \phi \le 120°$.

$$A = \int_0^5\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3} r\,d\phi\,dz = 5\pi \approx 15.7 \text{ m}^2$$
Problem 9 — Volume of a Sphere

Obtain the expression for the volume of a sphere of radius $a$ from the differential volume.

$$V = \int_0^a\int_0^\pi\int_0^{2\pi} r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi = \frac{4}{3}\pi a^3$$
L-06 / L-07

Electric Field Intensity & Charge Distributions

Electric Field Intensity

Consider a test charge $Q_t$ placed near a source charge $Q_s$. The electric field intensity is the vector force on a unit positive test charge:

Electric Field Intensity
$$\mathbf{E}_p = \frac{F_{ts}}{Q_t} = k\frac{Q_s}{r_{ts}^2}\hat{r}_{ts} = k\frac{Q_s}{r_{ts}^3}\vec{r}_{ts}$$ Unit: Volts per meter (V/m)
Eq. (22)–(23)
Key Properties of Electric Field
  • The direction of electric field does not depend on the sign of the test charge
  • The magnitude is independent of the magnitude of the test charge
Electric field radiates outward from a positive point charge — both full and sparse representations
Electric field vector map between two charges (orange = positive, blue = negative)

Superposition of Electric Fields

For $N$ charges located at $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N$, the total electric field at point $\mathbf{r}$ is:

Superposition Principle
$$\mathbf{E}(\mathbf{r}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{Q_i(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_i)}{4\pi\varepsilon_0|\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_i|^3}$$
Eq. (24)
▶ Interactive Simulation: Electric Field Lines
Click canvas to place charges | Drag to move
Problem 9 — Superposition

A charge of $-0.3\,\mu$C is located at $A(25,-30,15)$ cm, and a second charge of $0.5\,\mu$C is at $B(-10,8,12)$ cm. Find $\mathbf{E}$ at the origin.

$$\mathbf{E} = 92.3\hat{a}_x - 77.6\hat{a}_y - 94.2\hat{a}_z \text{ kV/m}$$

Continuous Charge Distributions

Electric field contribution $d\mathbf{E}$ from each charge element $dq$ at point $P$
TypeDensity SymbolUnitCharge ElementTotal ChargeElectric Field
Line Charge $\lambda$ C/m $dQ = \lambda\,dl$ $Q = \int_\ell \lambda\,dl$ $\displaystyle\mathbf{E}(P) = k\int_\ell \frac{\lambda\,dl}{r^2}\hat{r}$
Surface Charge $\sigma$ C/m² $dQ = \sigma\,dA$ $Q = \int_S \sigma\,dA$ $\displaystyle\mathbf{E}(P) = k\int_S \frac{\sigma\,dA}{r^2}\hat{r}$
Volume Charge $\rho_v$ C/m³ $dQ = \rho_v\,dV$ $Q = \int_V \rho_v\,dV$ $\displaystyle\mathbf{E}(P) = k\int_V \frac{\rho_v\,dv}{r^2}\hat{r}$
Problem 10 — Total Charge Calculations

(i) Line charge of infinite extent in $z$ with $\lambda = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{1+(z/a)^2}$

(ii) Line charge $\lambda_0$ uniformly distributed in a circular loop of radius $a$

(iii) Surface charge $\sigma_0$ uniformly distributed on a circular disk of radius $a$

(iv) Volume charge $\rho_0$ uniformly distributed throughout a sphere of radius $R$

(i) $Q = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{\lambda_0}{1+(z/a)^2}dz = \lambda_0 a \pi$

(ii) $Q = \lambda_0 \cdot 2\pi a$

(iii) $Q = \sigma_0 \cdot \pi a^2$

(iv) $Q = \rho_0 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$

L-08 / L-09

Electric Field: Line Charge, Sheet & Field Lines

Electric Field Due to Infinite Line Charge

For an infinite line charge along the $z$-axis with charge density $\lambda$ (C/m), the electric field at radial distance $\rho$ is found by integrating the contribution $d\mathbf{E}$ from each element $dz'$:

Field of Infinite Line Charge
$$d\mathbf{E} = \frac{\lambda\,dz'(\rho\hat{a}_\rho - z'\hat{a}_z)}{4\pi\varepsilon_0(\rho^2+z'^2)^{3/2}}$$ By symmetry, the $z$-components cancel. Integrating only $\hat{a}_\rho$: $$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\rho}\hat{a}_\rho}$$

The field points radially outward from the line and falls off as $1/\rho$.

Infinite line charge — field radiates radially

Electric Field Due to Infinite Sheet of Charge

For an infinite plane sheet with surface charge density $\sigma$ (C/m²):

Field of Infinite Sheet
$$d\mathbf{E} = \frac{\sigma\,\rho\,d\phi\,d\rho\,(h\hat{a}_z - \rho\hat{a}_\rho)}{4\pi\varepsilon_0(\rho^2+h^2)^{3/2}}$$ Integrating over the plane: $$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_N}$$
Important Result

The electric field from an infinite sheet is constant — independent of distance from the sheet!

Infinite sheet of charge — uniform field on both sides

Properties of Electric Field Lines

  • Field lines must begin on positive charges and terminate on negative charges (or at infinity for isolated charges)
  • The number of field lines leaving/entering a charge is proportional to the magnitude of the charge
  • The strength of the field is proportional to the closeness of field lines — proportional to the number of lines per unit area perpendicular to the lines
  • The direction of the electric field is tangent to the field line at any point in space
  • Field lines can never cross
(a) Single positive charge, (b) Single negative charge — equal magnitude, (c) Negative charge with twice the magnitude
Problem 10 — Zero Electric Field

A charge $Q_1 = -2\,\mu$C is at $x=0$, and $Q_2 = +8\,\mu$C is at $x = -0.5$ m.
(i) Find $\mathbf{E}$ for $x = -76.5$ m
(ii) At what point (besides $|x| = \infty$) is the electric field zero?

(i) $E = 9.389$ N/C

(ii) $x = -0.5$ m (between the charges, on the negative side) and $x = 1.5$ m (on the positive side, where the fields of the two charges cancel)

L-10

Divergence, Curl & Electric Flux Density

Divergence of a Vector

The divergence at a given point $P$ is the outward flux per unit volume as the volume shrinks about $P$:

Divergence (Definition)
$$\nabla\cdot\mathbf{D} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0}\frac{\oint_S \mathbf{A}\cdot d\mathbf{s}}{\Delta v}$$ The del operator: $\nabla = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$
Eq. (25)
Coordinate SystemDivergence $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{A}$
Cartesian$\dfrac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$
Cylindrical$\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial(\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \dfrac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$
Spherical$\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial(r^2 A_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial(A_\theta\sin\theta)}{\partial\theta} + \dfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial\phi}$
Divergence Theorem
$$\oint_S \mathbf{A}\cdot d\mathbf{s} = \int_V \nabla\cdot\mathbf{A}\,dv$$
Eq. (26)

Curl of a Vector

The curl of a vector $\mathbf{A}$ is an axial vector whose magnitude is the maximum circulation of $\mathbf{A}$ per unit area as the area tends to zero, and whose direction is normal to the area when the circulation is maximum:

Curl (Definition)
$$\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = \lim_{\Delta S\to 0}\left(\frac{\oint_L \mathbf{A}\cdot d\mathbf{l}}{\Delta S}\right)_{\max}\hat{a}_n$$
Eq. (27)
SystemCurl $\nabla\times\mathbf{A}$
Cartesian $$\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = \begin{vmatrix}\hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z\end{vmatrix}$$
Cylindrical $$\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{\rho}\begin{vmatrix}\hat{a}_\rho & \rho\hat{a}_\phi & \hat{a}_z \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial \rho} & \frac{\partial}{\partial \phi} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ A_\rho & \rho A_\phi & A_z\end{vmatrix}$$
Spherical $$\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\begin{vmatrix}\hat{a}_r & r\hat{a}_\theta & r\sin\theta\hat{a}_\phi \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} & \frac{\partial}{\partial\phi} \\ A_r & rA_\theta & r\sin\theta A_\phi\end{vmatrix}$$
Stokes' Theorem
$$\oint_L \mathbf{A}\cdot d\mathbf{l} = \int_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{A})\cdot d\mathbf{s}$$
Eq. (28)

Electric Flux Density (D)

In SI units, one line of electric flux emanates from $+1$ C and terminates on $-1$ C. The total electric flux $\psi$ coming out of a closed surface is:

Electric Flux & Flux Density
$$\psi = \oint_S \mathbf{D}\cdot d\mathbf{s}$$ The vector $\mathbf{D}$ is the electric flux density, measured in C/m².

Relation to electric field (in free space): $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}$$
Key Property

The electric flux density $\mathbf{D}$ is independent of the medium.

L-10 / L-11

Faraday's Experiment & Gauss's Law

Faraday's Experiment

Faraday placed a known charge $Q$ on an inner conducting sphere and surrounded it with an uncharged outer sphere. He observed that a charge of $-Q$ was induced on the outer sphere's inner surface.

This demonstrated the concept of electric flux: there was some "displacement" from the inner sphere to the outer sphere, which he called electric flux $\psi$.

Faraday's Key Result
$$\psi_{outer} = Q_{inner}$$
Faraday's experiment: inner sphere with charge $Q$, outer spherical Gaussian surface

Generalisation: Gauss's Law

Faraday's result generalises beautifully:

  • The flux passing through any closed surface located between two spheres equals the charge on the inner sphere
  • Even if the inner conductor is irregular, the induced charge on the outer sphere is the same
  • Changing the outer sphere to any irregular shape doesn't change the result
Gauss's Law (Statement)

"The electric flux passing through any closed surface is equal to the total charge enclosed by that surface."

Gauss's Law — Integral & Differential Form
$$\psi = Q_{enclosed} \implies \oint_S \mathbf{D}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = Q_{enc} \implies \oint_S \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$$
Applying the Divergence Theorem: $$\nabla\cdot\mathbf{D} = \rho_v \quad \text{(Differential form — Maxwell's 1st Equation)}$$
Application Procedure for Gauss's Law
  1. Identify whether a symmetric charge distribution exists
  2. Construct a mathematical closed surface (a Gaussian Surface) around the charge such that $\mathbf{D}$ is normal (or tangential) to the surface

Note: Gauss's law holds regardless of symmetry, but we can only use it to determine $\mathbf{E}$ or $\mathbf{D}$ when there is symmetric charge distribution.

Applications of Gauss's Law

1. Electric Field Due to a Point Charge

Point Charge — Gaussian Sphere of Radius r
$$\oint_S \mathbf{D}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = D_r\cdot 4\pi r^2 = Q$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{D} = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}\hat{a}_r \implies \mathbf{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r}$$

2. Electric Field Due to Infinite Line Charge (via Gauss's Law)

Line Charge — Gaussian Cylinder
$$\oint_S \mathbf{D}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = D_\rho\cdot 2\pi\rho l = \lambda l$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{D} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\rho \implies \mathbf{E} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\rho}\hat{a}_\rho}$$

3. Electric Field Due to Infinite Sheet of Charge (via Gauss's Law)

Sheet Charge — Gaussian Pillbox
$$D_z(A + A) = \sigma A \implies D_z = \frac{\sigma}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_z}$$ Electric field is independent of distance from the sheet!
Problem 12 — Two Charged Planes

Find the electric field in all regions of space where two charged planes with charges $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$ are separated by a distance $d$.

  • Region A (outside, above $+\sigma$): $E_A = 0$
  • Region B (between the plates): $E_B = E_{+\sigma} + E_{-\sigma} = \dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0} + \dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$
  • Region C (outside, below $-\sigma$): $E_C = 0$

4. Electric Field Due to Uniformly Charged Sphere

Electric field magnitude vs. distance for a uniformly charged sphere — peaks at surface ($r=R$), falls as $1/r^2$ outside
Insulating Sphere (Volume Charge $\rho_0$)
Inside ($r \le a$): $$D_r(4\pi r^2) = \rho_0 \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \implies \mathbf{D} = \frac{\rho_0 r}{3}\hat{a}_r$$ $$\mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho_0 r}{3\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_r$$
Outside ($r \ge a$): $$\mathbf{D} = \frac{\rho_0 a^3}{3r^2}\hat{a}_r, \quad \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho_0 a^3}{3r^2\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_r$$
Hollow (Shell) Sphere (Surface Charge $\sigma$)
Inside ($r \le a$): $$\mathbf{D} = 0, \quad \mathbf{E} = 0$$
Outside ($r \ge a$): $$\mathbf{D} = \frac{a^2\sigma}{r^2}\hat{a}_r, \quad \mathbf{E} = \frac{a^2\sigma}{r^2\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_r$$
▶ Simulation: Gauss's Law — E-Field vs. Distance
L-12

Electric Field in Metals & Electric Potential

Electric Field Inside a Metal Conductor

Key Result
$$\mathbf{E}_{inside} = 0$$

The electric field inside a metal conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is always zero. Any excess charge resides on the surface.

Potential Energy

The work done by an external agent (against the electric force) in bringing charge $q_2$ from infinity to distance $R$ from $q_1$ is:

Potential Energy
$$W = -\int_\infty^R \mathbf{F}_{+q_1}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \frac{q_1 q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R} \quad \text{(Joules)}$$ $$U = \frac{q_1 q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R}$$
Sign Convention
  • If $q_1$ and $q_2$ are both positive (or both negative): $W > 0$ — external agent must do work
  • If $q_1$ and $q_2$ have opposite signs: $W < 0$ — the field does the work
  • For a collection of charges: total work = sum of work done in bringing each charge from infinity
Bringing charge $q_2$ from infinity to point $R$ in the field of $q_1$

Electric Potential (Voltage)

The electric potential at a point is the work done per unit charge in bringing a unit positive test charge from infinity to that point:

Electric Potential of a Point Charge
$$V_P = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R} = -\int_\infty^R \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l}$$
Potential difference between points $A$ and $B$: $$V_{AB} = V_B - V_A = -\int_A^B \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_B} - \frac{1}{r_A}\right)$$

Equipotential Surfaces

Properties of Equipotential Surfaces
  • They are perpendicular to electric field lines
  • They never intersect each other
  • Work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero
  • They are closer together in regions of stronger electric field, and farther apart in weaker field regions
Equipotential circles (dashed) are perpendicular to the radial field lines of a point charge
Equipotential surfaces and field lines of an electric dipole — red (positive charge), blue (negative charge)

Relation Between E and V

E = −∇V (Maxwell's Equation)
$$dV = -\mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l} = -E_x\,dx - E_y\,dy - E_z\,dz$$
From vector calculus: $dV = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y}dy + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial z}dz$

Therefore: $E_x = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}$, $E_y = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y}$, $E_z = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial z}$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V}$$
Physical Interpretation
  • Electric field is the negative gradient of voltage
  • The negative sign indicates that $\mathbf{E}$ is in the direction of decreasing $V$
  • Electric field is directed from higher to lower voltage
  • Potential between two points: $V_{AB} = -\int_A^B \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l}$

Maxwell's Equation: $\oint \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l} = 0$

Conservative Nature of Electrostatic Field
$V_{AB} = -V_{BA}$, therefore $V_{AB} + V_{BA} = 0$: $$\oint_C \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l} = 0$$ Applying Stokes' theorem: $$\nabla\times\mathbf{E} = 0$$
Physical Meaning

This equation is the more general form of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for electrical circuits. It states that the electrostatic field is conservative — the work done around any closed loop is zero.

L-13

Electric Dipole

What is an Electric Dipole?

An electric dipole consists of a positive charge and a negative charge separated by a small distance. The entire object is electrically neutral.

Why Study Dipoles?
  • Everything around us is neutral — we rarely encounter isolated positive or negative charges
  • The dipole is the fundamental model for polarized molecules (like water)
  • Strength of dipole ⇒ electric dipole moment
Electric Dipole Moment
For charges $+q$ at $+d$ and $-q$ at $-d$ along $y$-axis: $$\vec{p} = q_1\vec{r}_1 + q_2\vec{r}_2 = -q a(-\hat{j}) + q a(\hat{j}) = 2qa\,\hat{j}$$ For system of charges with total charge zero: $$\vec{p} = q_1\vec{r}_1 + q_2\vec{r}_2 + \cdots + q_n\vec{r}_n$$

The dipole moment is independent of the choice of origin.

Electric dipole geometry showing $+q$ and $-q$ separated by $2d$, and distances $r_1, r_2, r$ to observation point $R$

Torque on a Dipole in External Electric Field

Torque on Dipole
Net force = $q\mathbf{E}_{ext} - q\mathbf{E}_{ext} = 0$ (no net translation), but there is a torque: $$\vec{\tau} = \sum_{j=1}^N \vec{r}_j \times q_j\mathbf{E}_{ext} = \left(\sum_j q_j\vec{r}_j\right)\times\mathbf{E}_{ext}$$ $$\boxed{\vec{\tau} = \vec{p}\times\mathbf{E}_{ext}}$$

Electric Field Due to a Dipole

Dipole Potential & Field
$$V = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) \approx \frac{Q(2d\cos\theta)}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$$
$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V = \frac{Qd}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}(2\cos\theta\,\hat{a}_r + \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta)$$
Multipole Comparison
Type$V$ varies as$E$ varies as
Monopole (point charge)$1/r$$1/r^2$
Dipole$1/r^2$$1/r^3$
Quadrupole (2 dipoles)$1/r^3$$1/r^4$
Octapole (4 dipoles)$1/r^4$$1/r^5$
ScholarNest
